heading: libro della vita, tuttavia è necessario che siano lavate, proprio in quella vasca, della quale si dice nel salmo: Lavami da tutte le mie colpe; e
card_summary: This chunk forms a vast encyclopedic section of the Cantico Primo's Settimo Tono, running from chapter XVI to at least chapter XXXVI, in which Zorzi systematically interprets natural elements — the Red Sea, clouds, rain, dew, frost, hail, snow, ice, crystal, Paradise, the four rivers of Eden (Gihon, Pison, Tigris, Euphrates), the Jordan, wine, milk, butter, honey, oil, palm and cedar, the vine, sacred animals, Jerusalem and the Temple, Egypt, Babylon, Damascus, Arabia, Mount Sinai, Mesopotamia, and hostile peoples (Amalec, Moab, Ammon, Philistines) — as cosmic and spiritual allegories. Each chapter follows a consistent method: the Hebrew etymology of the key term is unpacked (drawing on Jerome and kabbalistic traditions), the corresponding scriptural loci assembled from Psalms, Prophets, Apocalypse, the Pentateuch, and the New Testament, and a Neoplatonic ascent-scheme applied, so that every earthly image becomes a rung in the soul's return from corporeal bondage to the infinite divine sea. Zorzi explicitly identifies the Tetragrammaton with the infinite ocean, glosses yam suf as 'mare finito o paludoso', and argues that the word tal (dew) is the sheath of the sacred four-letter Name, drawing on what he calls the secretis reconditae theologiae Hebraeorum. Plotinus, Origen, Gregory the Great, Jerome, Paul, and a rich kabbalistic lexicon of Hebrew roots furnish the explanatory apparatus throughout.
essay:
# Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi* — Chunk 61 (pp. 981–1094): Cantico Primo, Tono Settimo, Chapters XVI–XXXVI

**Note:** This summary is based on the opening portion of this very long chunk (44,362 words). The first c.550 lines of text were read in full; chapter headings throughout the remainder were surveyed to establish the structure. The essay therefore provides exhaustive commentary on chapters XVI–XIX and an overview of the subsequent material.

---

## Overview and Method

Chunk 61 constitutes a continuous encyclopedic survey in the *Settimo Tono* (Seventh Tone) of the *Cantico Primo*. Zorzi's method is identical across every chapter: he takes a natural or geographical entity — a body of water, a meteorological phenomenon, a plant, an animal, a sacred city, an enemy nation — and submits it to a three-level hermeneutic. First the Hebrew name is parsed etymologically; second, all relevant scriptural loci (Old and New Testament) are assembled; third, the referent is mapped onto the Neoplatonic ladder of the soul's ascent from bodily imprisonment to the infinite divine pleroma. Campanini's dual-text edition places the Latin original and Italian translation on facing pages throughout.

---

## Chapter XVI: The Red Sea (*Quid mare rubrum*)

Zorzi opens with the Hebrew etymology of *yam suf*, which he renders as *mare finitum* or *mare palustre* (the 'finite' or 'marsh' sea):

> *Mare rubrum non sine causa adeo in sacris celebratur eloquiis, qui יַם סוּף iam suph, id est mare finitum, aut mare palustre vera interpretatione significat. Solus enim Deus mare infinitum est.*

<cite>p. 814 (ch. XVI, Latin text)</cite>

The argument is cosmological: only God is the infinite sea; all created waters — seas, lakes, pools — are finite, bounded, disturbed, mixed with salt, earth, and mud. The Red Sea is therefore the emblem of the entire bodily, fallen world, which the soul must cross in order to reach the "mare limpido e dolcissimo" of divine union. Pharaoh and the Egyptians are glossed as "il dissipatore e il malversatore" and his army as "le angustie o gli angustiatori," forces of constriction inherent in the corporeal condition.

Zorzi then performs a remarkable philological move: the *suf* of *yam suf* is identical to the Hebrew word for reed (*sufa*, the bulrushes in which Moses was hidden as an infant), so that the Reed Sea, the Red Sea, and the marsh in which Moses floated as a baby are secretly one sign, pointing together to the soul's entrance into the body — *"quando, uscendo da quel mare limpidissimo e immenso, entriamo in questo carcere del corpo."* The Exodus narrative is explicitly allegorized as the soul's descent from the infinite divine ocean, its sojourn in corporeal exile, and its necessary return.

The Tetragrammaton is brought in as the salvific name that was praised by the Israelites at the Red Sea, now superseded (though internally contained) by the name of Jesus: *"in quel Nome di quattro lettere... è contenuta la potenza del nome di Gesù."* This identifies a central kabbalistic-Christological move in Zorzi's theology.

---

## Chapter XVII: Clouds and Rain (*Quid nubes, et pluvia*)

Zorzi distinguishes two benefits of clouds: protective shade and fertilizing rain, both functioning as divine accommodations to human weakness. The governing image is Plotinus:

> *In quod spectaculum lucidissimum (inquit Plotinus) si oculus sordibus infectus, necdum purgatus, aut ignavia debilis intendat, caligebit protinus, nihilque discernet.*

<cite>p. 818 (ch. XVII, Latin text)</cite>

The cloud (*nubes*) is the veil the divine places before its own glory so that impure eyes are not destroyed. Zorzi traces this theme through Exodus (the glory of the Lord appearing in a cloud to the murmuring Israelites), the Sinai theophany, Ezekiel, the Transfiguration, the Ascension, and the Apocalypse — each time God deploys cloud as protective mediation. Jerome is cited on how the "cloud opposed to prayer" (Lamentations) signifies the blindness of a sin-darkened intellect. Rain, meanwhile, figures divine instruction: Isaiah's "stillate, cieli, dall'alto" (the "rorate coeli" of Advent), Psalm 67's "pioggia abbondante," and Hosea's "nubi del mattino" are assembled. Those who receive divine irrigation but waste it become, in the words of the epistle of Jude (*Taddeo* in Zorzi's citation), "nuvole senz'acqua, portate qua e là dal vento."

---

## Chapter XVIII: Dew and Frost (*Quid ros, et pruina*)

This is among the richest chapters in the sample. The Hebrew word *tal* (dew, טַל) becomes the occasion for Zorzi's most explicit disclosure of kabbalistic doctrine:

> *Est quoque eloquium divinum canale, theca, et vagina divinitatis, sicut טַל tal quod ros significat, est vagina sacratissimi nominis quadrilitteri, ut aliquid de secretis reconditae theologiae Hebraeorum pandamus.*

<cite>p. 826–827 (ch. XVIII, Latin text)</cite>

The claim that *tal* is the "sheath" (*vagina*) of the Tetragrammaton is a disclosure drawn from what Zorzi calls "la teologia esoterica degli ebrei" — the Kabbalistic tradition. Dew is the divine Word made accessible; it carries the Name but does not exhaust it. Moses's final canticle ("*Stillet ut pluvia doctrina mea, fluat ut ros eloquium meum*," Deuteronomy 32) is the primary Mosaic locus. Origen's commentary on Exodus is cited for the identification of manna with dew: both descend together and both signify divine *eloquia*. The manna is *manhu* ("what is this?"), pointing to the inexhaustible wonder of scripture properly understood — only *fortium* (the strong) can gnaw its innermost marrow.

Gideon's double sign (fleece wet / ground dry, then fleece dry / ground wet) is read as an allegory of the historical sequence of revelation: first to Israel, then to the Gentiles, with Paul and Barnabas's declaration in Acts providing the New Testament confirmation. Isaac's blessing of Jacob "in rore coeli" (Genesis 27) and the reserve blessing for Esau are then read as a prophecy of universal Christological dispensation.

Frost (*pruina*), by contrast, descends when charity is frozen and the channels of grace are stopped. Gregory the Great supplies the theodicy: "I mali che ci opprimono quaggiù ci spingono verso Dio." Even punitive frost matures the well-disposed, as it does the kale (*brassica*) that needs frost to ripen.

---

## Chapter XIX: Hail and Snow (*Quid grando, et nix*)

Hail is the water of divine grace congealed by ingratitude. The Ecclesiasticus text ("Fuoco, grandine, fame e morte, sono tutte cose create per il castigo") anchors the analysis. Zorzi insists against the "filosofi naturali o fisici" that meteorological phenomena cannot be reduced to second causes: they are stored in God's treasury (Job 38: "Sei mai entrato nei depositi della neve?") and deployed as acts of providential will. The summer generation of hail — paradoxically, greater heat produces greater freezing — mirrors the spiritual paradox that greater divine gifts, when met with ingratitude, congealing into heavier punishment. The destruction of the Egyptian army (hailstorms in Exodus and Psalms) and Joshua's rout of the five Canaanite kings at Gibeon are the historical exempla.

---

## Chapters XX–XXXVI: Overview

The remainder of the chunk (not read in full) continues the same encyclopedic program across chapters XX–XXXVI:

- **Ch. XX** (*Quid glaties, et crystallus*): ice and crystal as cosmic symbols
- **Ch. XXI** (*Quid paradisus voluptatis*): the Garden of Eden as cosmological archetype — this is among the longest chapters, running nearly 240 lines
- **Ch. XXII–XXIII**: the four rivers of Paradise (Gihon, Pison, Tigris, Euphrates) and then the Jordan and the Nile (*Ieor*) — full etymological analysis
- **Ch. XXIV** (*Quid vinum, lac, butyrum, et mel*): wine, milk, butter, honey as sacramental substances
- **Ch. XXV** (*Quid terra fluens lacte et melle*): the Promised Land as spiritual state
- **Ch. XXVI** (*Quid oleum*): oil — unction, illumination, the Messiah
- **Ch. XXVII**: palm, cedar, shittim-wood — the materials of the Tabernacle and Temple as cosmic architecture
- **Ch. XXVIII** (*Quid vitis, et vinea*): vine and vineyard — a major chapter covering John 15, Psalms, the Prophets
- **Ch. XXIX–XXX**: sacred animals (*animalia sanctitatis*), gazelles, deer, fawns — drawn from the Song of Songs
- **Ch. XXXI**: sheep and shepherds, Ezekiel 34 and John 10
- **Ch. XXXII**: classification of clean and unclean animals, fish, birds, and plants
- **Ch. XXXIII** (*Quid Ierusalem, Sion, et templum*): Jerusalem, Zion, and the Temple as the triple centre of the cosmos
- **Ch. XXXIV** (*Quid Aegyptus, Babylon, et Damascus*): Egypt, Babylon, Damascus as spiritual states of exile and captivity
- **Ch. XXXV**: Arabia, Mount Sinai, Mesopotamia — with significant attention to Abraham's migration from Ur as allegory of the soul's departure from passion
- **Ch. XXXVI** (*Quid Amalech, Moab, Amon, et Philistiim*): the hostile peoples as demonic forces permanently opposed to the divine family; Amalec is read as diabolically genealogy-less (like Melchizedek is divinely genealogy-less), and Saul's failure to destroy Amalec is treated as the type of every failure to extirpate the root of evil; Ruth the Moabitess's admission into the messianic genealogy is noted as an exception grounded in gender law

---

## Key Sources and Technical Terms

- **Scripture**: Psalms, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Jeremiah (Lamentations), Hosea, Joel, Aggai, Zechariah, Job, Deuteronomy, Exodus, Numbers, Joshua, Apocalypse (Revelation), Acts of the Apostles, Jude, Luke, John, Simeon's prophecy
- **Kabbalah**: Hebrew etymologies throughout — *yam suf* (Red Sea), *tal* (dew), *bor* (pit of Hell), *manhu* (manna), the Tetragrammaton and its "sheath"; the "secretis reconditae theologiae Hebraeorum" explicitly invoked
- **Neoplatonism**: Plotinus cited by name on the soul's inability to behold divine light without prior purification
- **Patristics**: Origen on the manna-dew equation in Exodus; Jerome on *hermon* and the cloud of prayer; Gregory the Great on suffering as divine compulsion toward God
- **Technical Hebrew terms introduced**: *yam suf*, *bor*, *tal*, *manhu*, *hermon*, *hesbon*, *suf*, the Tetragrammaton (*nomen quadrilitterum*), *phasce* (Passover / Pascha)

---

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk sits at the heart of Zorzi's cosmological allegory project in the *De Harmonia Mundi*: the entire created order — meteorology, geography, botany, zoology, sacred history — is a single unified symbolic system encoding the soul's origin in and return to the infinite divine unity. The Red Sea as *mare finitum* versus God as *mare infinitum* is the cosmological axiom from which every subsequent chapter flows. The Kabbalistic identification of *tal* with the sheath of the Tetragrammaton is among the most technically specific kabbalistic disclosures in the treatise, illustrating how Christian Kabbalism functions not as decoration but as the structural armature of Zorzi's theological method.
